english: sṅár-gyi rgyun (sngar gyi rgyun) all the time before, opp. to da-ltar (da ltar) now; also for ordinarily, predominantly, e.g. ordinarily it is white, only by way of exception it is of another colour; k'a-rgyún (kha rgyun) = k'a-rgyúd (kha rgyud) tradition; rgyún-gos (rgyun gos) an every day coat, opp. to yzáb-gos (gzab gos); rgyun-gág (rgyun gag), and more frq. rgyun-čád (rgyun chad) an interruption of flowing, of progress, hence rgyun-čád-méd-par (rgyun chad med par), or rgyun-mí-čád-par (rgyun mi chad par) uninterruptedly; rgyun-zás (rgyun zas) daily food; rgyun-ríṅ-ba (rgyun ring ba) lasting, of long continuance; rgyun-lám (rgyun lam) an endless, interminable way, to be pursued again and again, e.g. °kór-bai ('khor ba'i) of transmigration, byaṅ-čúb-kyi (byang chub kyi) of virtue, holiness Mil.

1. v. rgyu (rgyu). — 2. (Cs. rgyús-ma (rgyus ma)) notice, intelligence, knowledge, ṅá-la dé-i rgyus yod (nga la de'i rgyus yod) I am acquainted with it, I know the thing, I am up to it, frq; W.: *gyus yód-k'an* (gyus yod mkhan) one that knows about it; *gyus yód-pai lam* (gyus yod pa'i lam) a well-known road; ča-méd yúl-du rgyus-méd °k'yam (cha med yul du rgyus med 'khyam) as a stranger I am rambling through a foreign country Glr.; lo-rgyús (lo rgyus) 1. annals, chronicle, 2. in a general sense history, story, tale, narrative, lo-rgyús bśád-pa (lo rgyus bshad pa) to relate a story Glr., *ṅá-la lo-gyús śád-če máṅ-po yod* (nga la lo gyus bshad ce mang po yod) W. I have much to relate, to tell; lo-rgyús żíb-tu °drí-ba (lo rgyus zhib tu 'dri ba) to ask closely, to inquire minutely into a story Mil.; góṅ-gi lo-rgyús bśád-do (gong gi lo rgyus bshad do) he reported what was related above Pth.; also used of any short notice or intelligence, without reference to things past: der °byón-pai lo-rgyús gsuṅs (der 'byon pa'i lo rgyus gsungs) he mentioned that he was going there Mil.
the fine threads or fibres of which animal muscle, plants etc. are composed; rgyús-pa-čan (rgyus pa can) fibrous; rgyus-skúd (rgyus skud) catgut.

pf. brgyos (brgyos), fut. brgyo (brgyo), imp. rgyos (rgyos), to unite in sexual embrace. This word is an undisguised, and therefore somewhat obscene expression, which in books and in col. language is avoided, though referring to an act not criminal in itself, as Cs. seems to have understood it, when he translates rgyo-ba (rgyo ba) by: to abuse, constuprate, ravish: hence it is allowable, yet vulgar, to say: *'á-pa daṅ 'á-ma gyó-wa jhe'* ('a pa dang 'a ma gyo wa jhe) C.

pf. brgyaṅs (brgyangs), fut. brgyaṅ (brgyang), seems to be a secondary form of rkyóṅ-ba (rkyong ba), to extend, stretch, spread (vb. a.); the word is to be found in Lexx., but seems to be little used; brgyáṅs-pai má-tsa (brgyangs pa'i ma tsa) Pth. a disease consisting in some parts of the body being morbidly distended (?).
Cs., a secondary form of rgyáb-pa (rgyab pa), prob. but a provincialism.
also sga (sga), शृङ्गवेर ginger (fresh or dried); lga-rlón (lga rlon) fresh ginger.
Pth.: skya-lgaṅ-ṅé (skya lgang nge), is stated to mean: perfectly white.
Cs. = pa-ba-dyo-dyó (pa ba dyo dyo) puff-ball.
= rgyám-tsa (rgyam tsha) Zam., a kind of rock-salt.

1. gen. lča-sga (lca sga), bča-sga (bca' sga), ginger, = lga (lga); sga-skyá (sga skya) Lt. id. (?); sgá-pi-po (sga pi po) Lt. prob. for sga daṅ pi-pi-líṅ daṅ pó-ba-ri (sga dang pi pi ling dang po ba ri) ginger and two kinds of black pepper; sga-spyód (sga spyod) Sch. = sga-skyá (sga skya). — 2. saddle, rta-sga (rta sga) (Ld. *stásga* (sta sga)) horse-saddle; sga bstád-pa (sga bstad pa), resp. číbs-sga bstád-pa (chibs sga bstad pa) Glr., to lay the saddle on, to saddle; sga-k'ébs (sga khebs) saddle-cloth, Sch.: the leather cover or coating of a saddle; sga-gló (sga glo) saddle-girth W., C.; sga-lúg (sga lug) Cs.: frame of the saddle; saddle-bow, saddle-tree; sga-śá (sga sha) straps for fastening the travelling-baggage to the saddle, cf. śa-stág (sha stag) 2.

1. a projecting hill or spur, or the side of a larger mountain; sgaṅ-gśóṅ (sgang gshong) elevations and depressions on a hill-side, in Kun. sgaṅ-k'úl (sgang khul); sgaṅ-k'a-la yod (sgang kha la yod) (the village) is situated on a mountain-spur; *sgaṅ gyáb-na* (sgang gyab na) W. when you have passed round the extremity of the hill. — 2. ču- (chu-)
